title: Northbrook Observatory — Public astronomy in the Northern Highlands
description: A small public-research observatory in northern New England. Forty-inch Cassegrain reflector, monthly observing nights, school programs, and a darker sky than the coast.

sections.title: A small observatory under a darker sky.
sections.lede: We point a forty-inch Cassegrain reflector at the night sky on the second Friday of every month, and we tell anyone who shows up what we're looking at. Founded 1888. Still here.

background.alt: Wide editorial photograph of a starfield over an evergreen ridge.

sections.text: A public-research observatory on a wooded ridge 1,840 feet above sea level. The dome holds a forty-inch f/8 Cassegrain reflector built in 1953 and refurbished in 2018. The site is dark enough to read Bortle 3 most clear nights and Bortle 2 in winter, when humidity drops and the lake-effect cloud cover thins.

items.definition: 40 inches (1016 mm). Cassegrain, f/8 system. Two instrument ports for a CCD imager and a low-resolution spectrograph.
German equatorial mount, refurbished 2018 with a Software Bisque Paramount ME II head and a closed-loop encoder. Tracks at sidereal rate to better than 1 arcsecond over a 20-minute exposure.
Bortle 3 typical, Bortle 2 in dry winter conditions. Mean seeing 2.4 arcseconds FWHM. Mean transparency 0.86 mag/airmass.
One full-time director, two part-time observers, a board of seven, and a rotating cast of volunteer hosts on public nights.

sections.caption: The dome at twilight, mid-October. The shutter is open and the telescope is pointed at the Andromeda Galaxy.

sections.text: We publish a monthly sky report on the first of every month. It's a one-page PDF: where to look, when to look, what's worth waiting up for, what's worth getting up early for. Subscribe below and we'll mail it to you. We will not sell your address; we will not mail you anything else.

items.value: Saturn at opposition. Rings open 4.2°. Cassini Division visible.
items.hint: Doors at 21:30, dome opens at full dark.

items.value: Perseid meteors, early phase. Outdoor observing only; dome closed.
items.hint: Bring a chaise. The lawn is the show.

items.value: Perseid peak. Outdoor observing + dome rotation between M27, M57, and the Veil.
Harvest Moon — bright, no deep-sky. Lunar terminator tour + Jupiter as moon-set lets the sky darken.

sections.body: Somewhere, something incredible is waiting to be known.

sections.text: Edgar Northbrook built the original 12-inch refractor on this ridge in 1888 because the air was clear and the railroad ran near enough that the equipment could be hauled by team. He donated the observatory to the town of Northbrook in 1923. The 40-inch Cassegrain replaced the refractor in 1953 with NSF support. The building was electrified the same year. The dome motor was replaced in 1978, the floor in 1991, and the entire mount and control system in 2018. Most of the brass is original.

sections.caption: The lawn between the dome and the visitor entrance, an hour before a public night.

sections.title: Book a night.
sections.lede: Public observing is the second Friday of every month, free to attend. Group visits and school programs need to be scheduled in advance — we have a small staff and a finite dome.
